---
title: "Verification Bandwidth and Context Adequacy"
chapter_id: "verification-bandwidth-and-context-adequacy"
part_id: "planning-memory-reasoning-execution"
status: "conceptual"
draft_maturity: "v0.2 manuscript draft"
last_updated: "2026-07-02"
primary_sources:
  - "verification_bandwidth"
  - "vcm_public"
  - "spinoza"
  - "treellm"
  - "vcm_editable"
evidence_level: "argument"
claim_label: "Design rationale"
open_evidence_gaps:
  - "Four local raw-cache mappings are passage-reviewed; `vcm_editable` remains connector/source-note mapped. Support remains argument until contradiction-rate, distractor-resistance, adequacy-labeling, or escalation-harness tests exist."
  - "Protocol schema/fixture validation, the synthetic context admission/adequacy harness, and the Verification bandwidth contradiction probe are implemented; the new probe covers synthetic pairwise contradiction blocking, drafting-only inadequate context, summary-promotion rejection, dominant-distractor miss rejection, high-risk escalation rejection, mode-confusion rejection, ignored negative evidence, unidentified semantic units, and support-state-promotion rejection, but no model contradiction-rate, distractor-resistance, or adequacy-classifier benchmark has been run."
  - "No support-state promotion is implied by this drafting pass."
---

## Chapter status

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| Chapter ID | `verification-bandwidth-and-context-adequacy` |
| Part | Part II - Planning, Memory, Reasoning, and Execution |
| Status | conceptual |
| Manuscript maturity | v0.2 manuscript draft |
| Last updated | 2026-07-02 |
| Primary source records | `verification_bandwidth`, `vcm_public`, `spinoza`, `treellm`, `vcm_editable` |
| Claim label | Design rationale |
| Evidence level | argument |
| Source queue | primary: `verification_bandwidth`, `vcm_public`; supporting: `spinoza`, `treellm`; connector/recovery: `vcm_editable` |
| Source loading state | passage-reviewed local raw cache: `verification_bandwidth`, `vcm_public`, `spinoza`, `treellm`; connector/source-note mapped: `vcm_editable` |
| Test state | `context_adequacy_record.valid.json` passes protocol fixture validation with claim scope, context packet ref, context scope, risk tier, negative evidence, verification artifact refs, audit refs, support-state effect, and non-claims; `AsiStackProofs.VerificationBandwidth` implements finite adequacy/admission separation, inadequate-context support blocking, verification-adequacy route gates, and a contradiction-probe fixture bridge; `python3 scripts/validate_context_admission_adequacy.py` implements synthetic adequacy-label and escalation gates; `python3 scripts/validate_verification_bandwidth_probe.py` passes a deterministic synthetic fixture with two valid synthetic adequacy traces and seven expected-invalid controls at `experiments/verification_bandwidth/results/2026-07-02-local.json`; contradiction-rate and distractor-resistance benchmarks remain planned. |

## Drafting guardrail

Verification bandwidth is a claim-control boundary, not a measured model law. The repo validates a context adequacy record and a deterministic contradiction/adequacy fixture, but it has not run the proposed contradiction-rate experiment, measured attention interference, or formalized the two-body verification limit.

It follows transactions because a coherent, authorized context view can still be inadequate for the claim at hand. Memory correctness and verification adequacy are different gates.

::: {.asi-human-only}
## Human Reading Path

A coherent memory view can still be insufficient for verification work. The stack has to separate having context from being able to verify with it. It needs to know when the available context, attention, time, tools, comparison method, and reviewer capacity are adequate for the claim being made.

This is a warning against long-context theater. More text in the window does not automatically mean better reasoning. A governed system should record when verification bandwidth is too narrow and downgrade, defer, split, or escalate instead of sounding certain. The honest output may be an adequacy label, not a more confident answer. Adequacy is a budgeted property, not a mood.

Knowing that verification is underpowered is itself valuable evidence for the next decision.

Adequacy labels keep uncertainty actionable instead of merely uncomfortable. They tell the system when more text would not actually create more checkable understanding.

Verification improves when the system knows what it cannot afford to check, and adequacy becomes the honest substitute for impossible total review. Honesty beats impossible certainty because context limits are part of truth.
:::

## Problem

Long context does not automatically provide long-range reasoning or enough verification capacity.

The central distinction is between **generation capacity** and **verification bandwidth**. A model may have enough context to mention two documents, summarize them, or weave them into plausible prose while still lacking enough active workspace to compare the relevant semantic units in detail. Context is adequate only relative to a target claim, a set of semantic units, a verification task, and a declared risk level.

The layer receives context packets from VCM, claims from the reasoning layer, and risk labels from governance. It emits an adequacy state: sufficient for drafting, sufficient for local consistency checks, insufficient for verification, or escalated to tribunal/proof review. The output is not more prose. The output is a decision about whether the currently loaded context can support the kind of claim the system is about to make.

That decision is local to a claim. A packet can be adequate for writing a chapter summary and inadequate for promoting a safety claim. It can be adequate for checking one citation and inadequate for comparing two long specifications. It can be adequate for a low-risk planning note and inadequate for a governance decision. Adequacy is therefore not a property of context in general; it is a property of a context/claim/verification-mode triple.

## Why existing approaches are insufficient

Adding tokens can increase distractors, attention interference, and unverified summary burden.

The `verification_bandwidth` source frames this as a two-body problem: rigorous comparison requires enough effective workspace to hold the compared units together. If one document, codebase, or specification dominates the active workspace, it can suppress the ability to check another. If consistency is checked only through a chain of local summaries, non-adjacent contradictions can survive by transitive decay.

External evaluation work gives the adequacy test sharper edges. ALCE (`ext_alce_2023`) separates generated answers from citation support, CheckList (`ext_checklist_2020`) shows behavioral test matrices beyond aggregate accuracy, GPQA (`ext_gpqa_2023`) exposes hard-question oversight pressure, LongBench (`ext_longbench_2023`) and RULER (`ext_ruler_2024`) stress long-context claims, LongLLMLingua (`ext_longllmlingua_2023`) highlights compression tradeoffs, and Reluplex (`ext_reluplex_2017`) shows how property-specific verification can be narrower than a broad safety story. Verification bandwidth uses those baselines to require adequacy certificates, not to claim that the repository has run their evaluations.

RAG and long context are therefore necessary infrastructure, not proof. They help place material near the model, but they do not certify that the model jointly verified all relevant constraints. Tree-structured representation, VCM pages, and Spinoza-style claim ledgers are useful only if they expose what was compared, what was summarized, what was left as residual, and what claim class the packet is allowed to support.

The same warning applies to elegant diagrams, dense source notes, and high-quality summaries. They may improve retrieval and understanding, but they also create a temptation to confuse coverage with comparison. Verification bandwidth asks the system to name the comparison it actually performed.

## Core Claim

[verification-bandwidth-and-context-adequacy.core, label: Design rationale, support: argument] Context systems must distinguish generation capacity from verification bandwidth and declare when context is inadequate.

The claim remains at `argument` support. Verification Bandwidth supplies the generation-versus-verification distinction, semantic-unit comparison frame, transitive-decay risk, and proposed contradiction-rate test. VCM supplies adequacy/admission states, use-relative certificates, exact-decode versus semantic-materialization boundaries, and non-success vocabulary. Spinoza supplies claim-ledger consequences. TreeLLM supplies a representation route for explicit semantic units. Editable VCM remains a connector/source-note refinement source, not a local benchmark.

### Claim-source mapping status

Appendix C now maps this verification-bandwidth claim to all assigned source notes, and four local raw-cache mappings carry passage-reviewed references. The mappings support the distinction between generation capacity and verification adequacy, not an empirical law about model cognition or a completed contradiction benchmark.

| Source | What it supports | Limit |
|---|---|---|
| `verification_bandwidth` | Passage-reviewed: generation-versus-verification distinction, semantic units, effective verification workspace, coherence as constraint satisfaction, pairwise grinding, two-body verification limit, dominant-component suppression, transitive decay, interface-verification tradeoff, hierarchical summary fidelity tradeoff, proposed constraint-satisfaction/LCR test, and context window as verification bound. | Contradiction-rate, distractor-resistance, and adequacy-labeling experiments have not been run or mechanized here. |
| `vcm_public` | Passage-reviewed: object/version/representation/validation/packet/materialization identities, role-indexed operation contracts, adequacy/admission separation, strict SATISFIED/UNKNOWN/UNSATISFIED mapping, protected joint compilation, feasibility and semantic-fault outcomes, conservative recovery options, use-relative representation certificates, no-universal-strict-summary limitation, authority non-escalation, and evidence/authority separation. | No end-to-end model superiority, adequacy-classifier correctness, resolver, VCM-Bench result, or model-facing packet result exists. |
| `spinoza` | Passage-reviewed: proposer/verifier/maintainer separation, explicit formal-scope limits, structured unsupported states, verifier proof/refute/unknown outcomes, tier labels, proof budgets, solver status and counterexample/unsat-core reporting, contradiction-triggered belief revision, dependency tracing, entrenchment, retraction and re-verification, proof-carrying graph metadata, object-level tiering, proof invalidation, and reproducible-procedure support tiers. | Does not solve open-domain autoformalization, arbitrary theorem validity, verifier quality, or whole-system epistemic correctness. |
| `treellm` | Passage-reviewed: explicit traversable probabilistic graph/DAG proposal, concept/question/relation nodes, weighted edges, multi-entry roots, convergent paths, fixed semantic-token formats, path IDs, residual attributes, self-tokenizing bootstrap, reconstruction/alignment objectives, shared external DAG, incremental updates, explicit knowledge storage, memory-mapped lattice variants, and residual fingerprints. | No implementation, reasoning benchmark, fixed-token adequacy validation, graph coverage result, perfect-grounding claim, or model-size/performance claim exists here. |
| `vcm_editable` | Evidence-carrying planner-guided context compilation with virtual context ABI, representation graphs, certificates, authority non-escalation, transactional memory, and benchmark agenda. | Connector-readable refinement only; no VCM-Bench, contradiction benchmark, or citation-normalized external-literature support is recorded. |

## Mechanism

After ABI resolution, certificates, and transaction semantics produce an authorized context packet, Verification Bandwidth asks whether that packet can support the target claim. The Verification Bandwidth source supplies the generation-versus-verification distinction, semantic-unit comparison frame, two-body verification limit, crowd-out risk, transitive-decay risk, and contradiction-rate test agenda. VCM supplies adequacy/admission separation, use-relative sufficiency, non-success states, and the rule that a summary cannot recover omitted information without consulting a stronger source. Spinoza supplies claim-ledger consequences when verification is unknown or contradicted. TreeLLM supplies an explicit semantic-unit representation route, with the usual warning that representation format is not proof of adequacy. Editable VCM supplies the planner-guided context-compiler agenda as connector/source-note context only.

The mechanism turns context loading into an explicit adequacy contract. A context packet should identify the semantic units it claims to cover, the target claim or task, the compression path used to fit them, the loss or residual warnings, and the verification mode that was actually applied.

```{mermaid}
flowchart LR
  A["Source units"] --> B["VCM packet"]
  B --> C["Semantic-unit inventory"]
  C --> D["Compression and loss record"]
  D --> E{"Joint verification possible?"}
  E -- "yes" --> F["Adequate for target claim"]
  E -- "no" --> G["Inadequate or summary-only"]
  G --> H["Escalate retrieval or split review"]
  H --> I["Tribunal, proof, or human review"]
  F --> J["Bounded claim-ledger support"]
  I --> J
  J --> K["Residuals and downgrade path"]
```

**Reading the verification adequacy flow:** The adequacy flow asks whether the loaded packet can jointly verify the target claim, not merely whether more context was supplied. If joint verification is impossible, the route moves to retrieval, split review, tribunal, proof, or human review while support remains bounded.

The adequacy record models verification as constrained by semantic-unit coverage, representation quality, compression path, attention budget, and the actual comparison mode applied. Every context packet should carry an adequacy label for the target claim or task. High-risk claims route to stronger verification, split context, retrieval, tribunal review, proof work, or human review instead of receiving more context and a stronger-sounding answer.

The adequacy rule is deliberately blunt: if the system cannot name the semantic units that must be jointly checked, it cannot claim verification. If the units are too large for joint checking, the system may still draft, summarize, or propose a claim, but the claim ledger must mark the verification state as inadequate, summary-derived, or escalated.

### Adequacy states

| Adequacy state | Meaning | Allowed use |
|---|---|---|
| `absent` | Required source units are not loaded or not identified. | No source-derived claim. |
| `drafting_only` | Enough context exists to write or orient, but not to verify the target claim. | Prose draft, source queue, residual. |
| `local_check` | A narrow fact, citation, schema, or local consistency relation was checked. | Bounded claim with narrow support note. |
| `joint_check` | Required semantic units were compared in the declared mode. | Candidate for support promotion if other gates pass. |
| `summary_derived` | The claim rests on summaries or compressed packets with known loss. | Cannot exceed the summary's evidence ceiling. |
| `escalated` | The context is inadequate for the claim and has been routed to retrieval, split review, tribunal, proof, or human review. | No promotion until the escalation returns evidence. |
| `contradicted` | The adequacy pass found a conflict that matters to the target claim. | Revision, downgrade, or refutation path. |

This table does not measure a model's true attention. It gives the stack a public vocabulary for refusing overconfident prose.

## Interfaces

Context adequacy is carried by a compact adequacy record that travels with the context packet and the claim it supports.

- VCM declares adequacy.
- Spinoza checks claims.
- Planning decides escalation when adequacy is insufficient.

Minimum fields:

- `adequacy_id`
- `target_claim_id`
- `claim_scope`
- `context_packet_ref`
- `context_scope`
- `risk_tier`
- `semantic_units`
- `compression_path`
- `verification_mode`
- `adequacy_state`
- `residual_risks`
- `negative_evidence`
- `required_escalation`
- `verification_artifact_refs`
- `audit_refs`
- `support_state_effect`
- `non_claims`

Planning consumes the adequacy state before assigning review effort. VCM uses it to decide whether retrieval, summarization, or splitting is required. Spinoza-style claim ledgers use it to block support-state promotion when the context packet is adequate for drafting but not for verification.

An adequacy record also names the comparison mode:

- direct source lookup
- pairwise semantic-unit comparison
- schema or fixture validation
- proof-checker result
- tribunal/adversarial review
- human review
- summary-only review

Naming the mode prevents an adequacy state from becoming vague confidence. A claim may be locally checked by schema validation and still be unverified as an empirical claim. A proof-checker result may verify a finite predicate and still leave open whether the predicate models the real system. Mode-tagged adequacy preserves that difference.

## Invariants

- Adequacy is explicit.
- More context is not treated as proof.
- High-risk claims pay verification tax.
- Adequacy is scoped to a target claim and verification mode.
- Summary-derived support cannot exceed the declared loss and residual record.

These invariants are deliberately weaker than a theorem about model cognition. The book does not claim to know a model's true internal workspace. It claims that a governed stack should expose the boundary conditions under which a claim was produced, and should refuse to treat context length as evidence when the comparison task was not actually performed.

A refusal to verify under too little bandwidth is a valid outcome, not a failure of usefulness.

## Failure modes

- Attention interference.
- False confidence from large context.
- Verification skipped for cost reasons.
- Adequacy laundering, where a context packet adequate for drafting is reused as if it were adequate for support promotion.
- Mode confusion, where a local schema or proof check is treated as an empirical or deployment result.

Three failures deserve special handling. First, a dominant source can crowd out a smaller but decisive contradiction. Second, a summary can preserve topic coverage while dropping the exact constraint that matters. Third, a chain of locally consistent summaries can conceal a global contradiction. Dominance, summary-loss, and local-consistency failures emit residual records, not merely lower confidence scores.

Verification debt rollover turns skipped checks into apparent acceptance when they are repeatedly deferred.

## Minimum Viable Implementation

A context adequacy rubric and verification-bandwidth warning states.

The first adequacy implementation is deliberately narrow: a context adequacy rubric and warning-state schema. A toy fixture can give the system two or three documents with planted cross-document contradictions, then compare ordinary long-context drafting against a final explicit pairwise checking pass. The expected artifact is not a performance claim; it is a contradiction ledger showing which claims were checked, missed, escalated, or left unsupported.

The existing artifact is narrower: a `context_adequacy_record` schema and fixture. It records adequacy identity, target claim, claim scope, context packet ref, context scope, risk tier, semantic units, compression path, verification mode, adequacy state, residual risks, negative evidence, escalation requirements, verification artifact refs, audit refs, support-state effect, and non-claims, but it does not measure verification performance.

The Verification bandwidth contradiction probe is the next executable slice. `python3 scripts/validate_verification_bandwidth_probe.py` writes `experiments/verification_bandwidth/results/2026-07-02-local.json` and checks two valid synthetic adequacy traces plus seven expected-invalid controls. The valid traces cover a pairwise contradiction that blocks verified support and a drafting-only packet that remains inadequate for verification. The invalid controls reject summary-derived promotion, dominant-distractor contradiction misses, high-risk inadequate context without escalation, schema-mode support for empirical runtime claims, ignored negative evidence, verified support with unidentified semantic units, and support-state promotion from the fixture. This is a deterministic synthetic contradiction and adequacy fixture only; it creates no support-state transition, no evidence transition, no model-verification-bandwidth result, and no distractor-resistance benchmark.

## Beyond the State of the Art

Verification bandwidth becomes mature when it functions as a capacity-control plane. It does not reward a system for loading more text. It asks whether the loaded semantic units, representations, tools, reviewers, and comparison mode are strong enough for the exact claim, risk level, and support-state effect being requested.

When verification capacity is governed, every important claim has an adequacy record before it receives a stronger support label. The record names the source units, compression path, verification mode, negative evidence, residual risks, and escalation route. A context packet adequate for orientation can remain drafting-only; a packet adequate for one local citation can still be inadequate for a cross-document safety claim; a proof check over a finite predicate can stay separate from an empirical deployment claim.

A verification control plane needs:

- Adequacy classifiers scoped to target claim, risk tier, context packet, semantic-unit set, compression path, and verification mode.
- Warning states for absent context, drafting-only context, local checks, joint checks, summary-derived support, escalated review, and contradicted claims.
- Contradiction-rate and distractor-resistance test suites that measure when longer context hides rather than resolves conflicts.
- Escalation routes to stronger retrieval, split-context review, Spinoza proof envelopes, UAT review, human review, or residual preservation.
- Claim-ledger gates that block support-state promotion when the verification mode is narrower than the claim being made.

This verification-budget layer is still a target architecture. Verification-bandwidth support remains `argument` until adequacy classifiers, contradiction suites, escalation traces, and claim-ledger promotion gates show that the stack treats context length as exposure, not epistemic strength, and records when verification is insufficient.

## Codex test plan

| Test | Purpose | Status |
|---|---|---|
| Context adequacy fixture validation | Check that the adequacy fixture matches the public schema and declares target claim, claim scope, context packet, context scope, risk tier, semantic units, compression path, verification mode, adequacy state, residual risks, negative evidence, escalation requirements, verification artifact refs, audit refs, support-state effect, and non-claims. | implemented by protocol validation; validated locally |
| Distractor resistance test | Compare contradiction detection when a decisive small unit is paired with a dominant distractor unit. | planned; not run |
| Adequacy labeling test | Check that drafting-adequate context is blocked from verification-backed support when pairwise checking is absent. | implemented in `python3 scripts/validate_context_admission_adequacy.py`; no model verification-bandwidth claim |
| Verification escalation test | Check that high-risk claims with inadequate context route to tribunal, proof, retrieval, split-context, or human review instead of promotion. | implemented in `python3 scripts/validate_context_admission_adequacy.py`; no deployed escalation claim |
| Verification adequacy route proof | Check that a finite verification-adequacy review routes missing claims, unadmitted context, high-risk inadequate context, missing pairwise checks, missing verification artifacts, open negative evidence, contradictions, residuals, complete verified-support reviews, and complete draft-support reviews to explicit outcomes. | implemented by Lean build; finite verification-adequacy route coverage only |
| Verification bandwidth contradiction probe | Check a synthetic adequacy fixture covering pairwise contradiction blocking, drafting-only inadequacy, summary-derived promotion rejection, dominant-distractor miss rejection, high-risk inadequate context without escalation, schema-mode empirical support rejection, ignored negative evidence, unidentified semantic units, and support promotion from the fixture. | implemented in `python3 scripts/validate_verification_bandwidth_probe.py` with two valid synthetic adequacy traces and seven expected-invalid controls; no model-verification-bandwidth, contradiction-rate-performance, distractor-resistance, adequacy-classifier, deployed-claim-ledger, escalation-service, support-state-promotion, or evidence-transition claim |
| Mode-confusion audit | Check that schema, proof, summary, human-review, and empirical modes are not used to support broader claims than they actually verify. | planned; not run |

The fixture-shape check, finite-record proof predicates, synthetic context admission/adequacy harness, and deterministic contradiction-probe fixture are implemented. The finite route proof records how modeled adequacy reviews should route, and the contradiction probe checks local fixture boundaries, not whether a model actually has enough verification bandwidth. Distractor resistance, contradiction-rate measurement, semantic adequacy classification, and deployed claim-ledger outcomes still require logged prompts or deterministic checkers, real or replayed model traces, and reviewable claim-ledger results.

### Formalization hooks

| Tag | Module | Target | Status |
|---|---|---|---|
| `lean:verification_bandwidth.adequacy.operational_invariant` | `AsiStackProofs.VerificationBandwidth` | A context packet admitted for use may still be marked inadequate for a target claim. | implemented |
| `lean:verification_bandwidth.adequacy.failure_blocks_promotion` | `AsiStackProofs.VerificationBandwidth` | A high-risk claim with inadequate context cannot receive a verified support label. | implemented |
| `lean:verification_bandwidth.adequacy.route_envelope` | `AsiStackProofs.VerificationBandwidth` | A structured verification-adequacy review routes missing claims, unadmitted context, high-risk inadequate context, missing pairwise checks, missing verification artifacts, open negative evidence, contradictions, residuals, complete verified-support reviews, and complete draft-support reviews into explicit outcomes. | implemented |
| `lean:verification_bandwidth.contradiction_probe_fixture_bridge` | `AsiStackProofs.VerificationBandwidth` | The deterministic synthetic contradiction probe result includes a contradiction-blocking pairwise trace, a drafting-only inadequate-context trace, rejected negative controls, no support-state effect, and an explicit non-claim boundary. | implemented |

These Lean hooks are implemented as finite-record predicates over context-packet, claim-support, verification-adequacy reviews, and a contradiction-probe summary. They prove only the record-level gates: admission and adequacy are distinct states, a high-risk claim with inadequate context cannot receive a verified support label, a modeled adequacy review routes invalid, residual, draft-support, or verified-support states explicitly, and the deterministic probe summary carries the required non-claim boundary. They do not prove a model's internal verification bandwidth, contradiction-rate performance, distractor resistance, or escalation quality.

## Source crosswalk

| Source ID | Title | Layer | Planned use | Readiness |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `verification_bandwidth` | Verification Bandwidth in Bounded Contexts | context_verification_theory | Generation vs verification; long context is not automatically long-range reasoning. | source note available; local raw cache passage-reviewed |
| `vcm_public` | Virtual_Context_Memory_v1 | memory_context | Public VCM release. Governed protocol for compiled working context. | source note available; local raw cache passage-reviewed |
| `spinoza` | Proof of Belief / The Spinoza Architecture | reasoning_epistemology | Neurosymbolic belief, transparent axiomatic AI belief systems, verification, belief revision. | source note available; local raw cache passage-reviewed |
| `treellm` | TreeLLM | semantic_representation | Hierarchical semantic token system for grounded, efficient, explainable language modeling. | source note available; local raw cache passage-reviewed |
| `vcm_editable` | Virtual_Context_Memory_v1.0_Editable | memory_context | Editable version with evidence-carrying planner-guided context compiler framing. | source note available; connector or recovery required |

The assigned sources supply the context-adequacy and verification-capacity vocabulary. Four local mappings are passage-reviewed, while `vcm_editable` remains connector/source-note mapped. The crosswalk does not claim the proposed contradiction-rate experiment has been run.

## Summary

Verification Bandwidth and Context Adequacy is the stack's refusal to equate access with understanding. A system may hold the right documents, have enough context length, and still lack the time, comparison method, tool support, or reviewer bandwidth needed to verify the claim it is about to make.

The design principle is conservative: context can be present, relevant, and still inadequate for the claim being made. A living ASI stack should therefore record adequacy as a first-class state, route high-risk claims to stronger verification, and treat missed contradictions as residuals that improve the next context compiler. Source review supports the control boundary, not a measured verification-bandwidth law. This closes the context substrate and hands the book to the claim-ledger chapters, where adequacy records become belief-revision inputs rather than background notes.

## Handoff

Adequacy records need somewhere durable to land, otherwise verification limits remain comments around the work rather than part of the work. Claim Ledgers and Belief Revision turns those limits into claim identity, evidence state, contradiction links, revision history, uncertainty, and required promotion conditions. The stack can then change belief without erasing why the earlier belief was weaker.
